name: Hillhouse Capital
shortDescription: China's largest healthcare-focused growth investor; anchor checks across the 2018–2021 HKEX 18A wave and BeiGene/Innovent/Junshi/Akeso scaling.
narrativeHook: Lei Zhang took $30M from Yale's endowment in 2005 and built it into the most prolific anchor investor in Chinese biotech: Hillhouse pre-IPO checks underwrote BeiGene, Innovent, Junshi, and Akeso, and the firm publicly described biotech as one of three permanent core practices through the 2022 cycle bottom.

investorContext.hookFact: Hillhouse anchored a meaningful share of HKEX 18A IPOs from 2018 onward and made biotech one of the firm's permanent core practices through the 2022 funding bottom.

name: Qiming Venture Partners
shortDescription: Early-stage VC with a deep healthcare practice; backed Innovent, Zai Lab, GenScript, Tigermed, CanSino at Series A or earlier.
narrativeHook: When Gary Rieschel and Duane Kuang launched Qiming in 2006, the cross-border venture template was being copied from Sand Hill Road — but Qiming was the firm that consistently wrote the first $5–15M check into the founders who would define the next decade of Chinese biotech.

investorContext.hookFact: Qiming managed roughly $9.5B across multiple funds by 2024, with healthcare consistently 25–35% of deployment.

name: Lilly Asia Ventures
shortDescription: Eli Lilly's spinout China-focused biotech VC; portfolio includes BeiGene, Innovent, Junshi, Tigermed, Zai Lab.
narrativeHook: Born inside Eli Lilly's APAC corporate-venture arm in 2008 and operationally independent since, LAV under Yi Shi has been an unusually consistent Series A presence — its anchor checks into Innovent, Junshi, BeiGene, and Zai Lab span essentially the entire innovator-generation cohort.

investorContext.hookFact: LAV is one of the very few biotech-dedicated VCs in Asia with a multi-decade deployment track and has remained operationally independent of Eli Lilly's corporate strategy.

name: HongShan (formerly Sequoia China)
shortDescription: Originally Sequoia Capital China; split from Sequoia in 2023 and rebranded HongShan in 2024. Healthcare is one of three core practices.
narrativeHook: After Sequoia Capital's parent severed its three regional arms in June 2023 over US-China geopolitical risk, Neil Shen took the China business independent and rebranded it HongShan ('redwood') in 2024 — making explicit what had effectively been the case for a decade: China venture capital is a fully separate ecosystem from Silicon Valley's.

investorContext.hookFact: The June 2023 Sequoia split (US/Europe → Sequoia Capital, India/SE Asia → Peak XV, China → HongShan) was an explicit acknowledgement that cross-Pacific venture capital had become geopolitically untenable.
